text: Good morning, Vip. It's Geoff Coghlan here from the Wesley Hospital in Brisbane, Australia, and I've got John Yaxley here with me.
Hi, Vip. Congratulations on your ten-thousandth case. It's a wonderful effort. Congratulations.
Yeah, Vip, it's great to be part of your day today. We're very proud of your achievements and all you've done for our specialty and robotics internationally.
Congratulations. And we also wanted you to invite us to present this case of Geoff's. Thank you.

is a young man, he's fifty-two, PSA at five point seven, he's got palpable disease at the left apex and mid regions of his prostate and has high volumes of Gleason four plus three adenocarcinoma in that area and the rest of his prostate's clear on biopsy.
And we have a PSMA PET scan here that shows uptake in that region with no evidence of disease elsewhere.
And we'll just start our broadcast from now after the bladder's been taken down.

So Jeff, just starting off, do you have any particular area where you cut the endopelvic fascia? Some people do a very high incision, almost like a nerve-sparing incision into the fascia versus the standard incision.
Just for people starting out, what do you think about that?

The surgeon makes a lateral incision at the base of the prostate, targeting the space between the prostate and levator muscles. The incision progresses through two layers to expose the raw red muscle fibers of the levator ani.
And I know other people stay closer to the prostate, but I do like this plane and get a good view of the prostate and good mobility of the prostate at the start.

Cutting the pubic prostate ligaments, do you think it's better to do that early or do you think it doesn't matter when you do that? Look, they're going to get cut at some stage in the case, so at this point I normally leave them intact and will divide through that area with the dorsal venous complex, but I don't think there's really a significant difference in when they're divided or at what point.

So going through the bladder neck now, for people who are watching or just starting their robotic careers, any little special techniques about finding the exact position in the bladder neck where you're going to cut? I noticed your technique of bunching there, but have you got any comments about that or any other techniques?

The surgeon opens the endopelvic fascia, using a pinch maneuver with both hands to assess the contour of the prostate gland's base. The catheter balloon is visible, but the surgeon prefers tactile feedback over visual cues from the balloon or fat removal.

The majority of my cases I'm not looking to spare the bladder neck aggressively and I'm happy to leave detrusor fibers all over the bladder neck. I'm not convinced there's a big continence advantage in an aggressive bladder neck preserving technique.

The surgeon uses bipolar or monopolar cautery to control vessels in the anterior part of the bladder, ensuring to stay in the midline to avoid larger veins that are more challenging to manage with heat.
So assistant-wise, you've got a nurse practitioner assistant. Do you have any comments on nurse practitioner's assisting?

Look, when I was with VIP and he had a physician's assistant that he'd done a lot of cases with, I realised early on that I succeeded well with her assisting me and not as well if I had a new fellow working with me. And the importance of a skilled assistant was obvious.

So the easiest way to get an ongoing skilled assistant nursing staff and Sheree who's assisting me today we've done probably fifteen hundred cases together and I'm certainly most comfortable operating with her.

So now you've gone through the anterior blood neck what's your approach from here Jeff? Look, from here, I guess different to what I learned with VIP, my approach is going to be to do the apical dissection first. And I approach it from the side that I'm going to nerves there where there's no disease. And my aim is going to be to do a high release of the right neurovascular bundle.

The surgeon visualizes the apical anatomy, performs sharp dissection to divide the dorsal vein, and then divides the urethra. The apex is lifted, and the surgeon plans an incremental left-sided nerve-sparing approach due to concerns about T-III disease.

The surgeon has cut through the initial part of the fascia and opened up the prostatic fascia. They are staying just inside the vein, aiming to leave a fascial layer on the prostate, and are currently in the desired interfascial plane.

The surgeon attempts a high release around the apex of the prostate, aiming to divide the urethra and dorsal vein. The anterior apex of the prostate is exposed, and the assistant uses a sucker to retract and apply pressure to manage any bleeding from the dorsal vein.
The surgeon encounters venous bleeding while performing the prostatectomy, focusing on identifying subtle visual cues to maintain the correct surgical plane.

So now we're doing the non-nurse spray on this side. Take me through your decisions on incremental versus total resection of the neurovascular bundle. Look, I base that decision on the grade of their disease, the volume of the disease, the findings on imaging.

So this patient has a reasonable amount of capsular abutment on his MRI, but no obvious extra capsular extension. So I'm concerned he might have a small amount of extra capsular extension and be a good candidate for partial nerve sparing. Those with higher grade disease, bulkier disease on examination and imaging, then I'll do a full neurovascular bundle resection on that side.
The surgeon approaches the dorsal venous complex, preparing to divide it. The complex is visible, and the decision is made whether to tie and ligate it before cutting.

I don't think there's a big difference and it really comes down to surgeon preference we see Vip has done ten thousand cases tying off the dorsal vein and he does a lovely apical dissection I personally have found if I tie off the dorsal vein suture ligated that I'm more likely to over dissect the apex and get worse early continence whereas I find this technique in the vast majority of patients allows me to identify the anatomy better and I think it gives me better early continence but I think it's really one of surgeon preference and experience.

The surgeon performs a direct division of the dorsal venous complex (DVC) while nerve sparing the right side. The dissection proceeds from lateral to medial around the apex of the prostate, sharply dissecting the dorsal vein to identify the anatomical landmarks of the prostate's apex.

Whereas if I come straight down from medial, I'm not as comfortable where the apex of the prostate is and I'm concerned that I'll either end up in the prostate or I'll be too aggressive on the dissection.
And what suture material are you going to use now for the DVC ligation? I normally use a six inch V-lock.

I would like to have some single arm quill sutures, but we don't have them at present. It's really one of the only places in the operation where I use V-lock. I find it's a bit rough to use on the urethra, but it's quite simple to use for the dorsal vein. Well, we've got a bit of time.

This patient has not... You've decided not to do a lymph node dissection on this patient. Any thoughts on which lymph node... which patients you perform pelvic lymph node dissections on and whether you perform an extended or a standard node dissection?

Yeah, node dissections, an ongoing debate, really, I think, and I have no doubt that it increases the morbidity of the operation. I only do extended node dissections. Mm-hm. I think its main benefit is as a staging procedure. So I will do it in young patients with high-risk disease or if it's someone like this with Gleason four plus three, I'll typically only do it if their PSA is much higher. I think my chances of getting positive nodes on him are low and I'm less likely to do it on people that are older where I'm very unlikely to use salvage or adjuvant radiotherapy. Once patients get to seventy, I'm generally, if I do a radical prostatectomy, that would be the only local treatment I give them.

So the staging benefits of knowing nodal status in that situation, I think, are less. And how has the PET-PSMA scan influenced your decision on node dissection? We do use a lot of PET-PSMA staging here in Brisbane.

Look, PET-PSMA is certainly an advance in staging of disease. I think what we're seeing internationally and certainly at our centre with a big experience is it's a status but its sensitivity is poor so certainly if we see positive nodes on the PSMA we will usually see much more significant nodal involvement histologically than we see radiologically but negative nodal staging on PSMA is not particularly reassuring in high-risk disease where we still have a reasonable percentage of patients returning positive nodes with a node dissection.

The surgeon begins ligating the dorsal venous complex (DVC), proceeding from lateral to medial and posterior to anterior. The ligation is performed with superficial sutures, starting from the right-hand side and moving across from front to back.

The surgeon addresses a bleeding artery in the mid-prostate region, using diathermy to cauterize significant arteries encountered during the procedure. If a venous sinus on the left side is bleeding due to high venous pressures, the surgeon prioritizes controlling bleeding from that side first.

well controlled with pneumoperitoneum i just start from the right and work across because it's the easiest way for me to do it and do you increase the neuroperitoneum at this point no i never i did early on but now i never do i'm a big believer in just reduce both for myself and the team.

And what pressure do you work at in the pneumoperitoneum? In patients like this that are young and fit, I work with it on fifteen. If they're older patients or I'm a bit worried about them in some way, I'll drop it to ten and I don't find a big difference in the bleeding at that point.

So if you can explain what you're doing here, Jeff, with this anterior bladder. Yeah, so I'll put a suture through this uracus. I did this at the very beginning of my consultant experience after my fellowship with VIP, really just to try and reduce the burden on my assistant when my assistant was inexperienced. useful, it's simple and quick to do and we also use a long lateral assistant port and if you pull this suture back through the right upper quadrant assistant port you get a nice bridge over the peritoneal contents with a long assistant port and I feel it's a bit safer when your assistants are traversing the abdomen out of vision. Talking about instruments, we've got two robots at our institution, an SI and an XI. Any preference for prostates between those technologies? I love the XI. The SI is my least favourite of the three robots that I use. I prefer to be on the S than this. I don't use the SI very much, only a few times a year. Today is unfortunately one of those days, but I'd much rather be on the XI.

So do you ever have to sew up the anterior, so the dorsal venous complex over the top of the prostate? Do you have general principles to leave it unless it's bleeding a lot? I leave it in virtually everyone now. When I started this technique and I'd moved from tying it off, I would typically put a backbleeder suture in, but I find that it's completely unnecessary in everyone.

The surgeon encounters an adhesion that has caused difficulties earlier in the operation. Using robotic assistance, they attempt to address the adhesion, noting the dexterity of the XI robot in maneuvering within the surgical field.

be a little bit annoying with the SI. So we do have patients that have had other interoperative procedures prior to their robotic prostatectomy. Any general principles about entry? vesiports etc when would you just try how do you enter that initially and when do you use a vesiport anyone who's had significant intra-abdominal surgery i'll always go in an upper quadrant with a opti-view vesiport if they've just had an appendix or something of the like i'll still normally do an umbilical hassan like i have in this patient And patients who've had intraperitoneal catastrophes with surgery, I'll usually bowel prep those patients. And those patients in salvage cases are usually the only ones that I would bowel prep.

The surgeon performs apical dissection from the nerve-sparing side, having completed the high release and divided the dorsal vein for increased prostate mobility. The Denonvilliers' fascia is visible at the back, and the surgeon works to separate the rectum from the majority of the prostate apex.

The surgeon navigates through the plane around the prostate, with the neurovascular bundle being pushed back to expose the apex of the prostate at its junction with the urethra. A fascia layer remains over the prostate, indicating the desired surgical plane.

But if it's sticky at that point, I certainly won't push it and try and get any more distance at all. And what sort of margin do you like to give for the urethra from the apex a couple of millimetres or what are your thoughts about intraprostate apical dissection to try and improve continence? I'm not convinced about intraprostatic dissection of the urethra. I think that probably does improve taking frozen sections and using that to determine their positive margin status. I'm not sure that's right. Patients who have apical disease, I like to leave a couple of mils on. And if there's not significant apical disease, I'll generally take it right flush with the prostatic apex.

If you were doing a bilateral nerve sprain, would you have freed this left side first before you cut through the urethra? Yes, I would have done identical dissection like I did there on the right. When I'm resecting some of the nerve bundle, I typically like to approach it from the contralateral side like I have here. And I actually circled the urethra with my left hand when I divided it. That's something I wouldn't do in a bilateral nerve sprain.
The surgeon divides the capsular artery, using it as a guide for nerve sparing. Small Asculap metallic titanium clips are applied to control bleeding from the divided artery near the urethra.

The surgeon uses a Raytech sponge to manage minor bleeding while dissecting through the bladder neck. The prostate is now highly mobile, which affects the ease of posterior bladder neck dissection compared to the anterior approach performed earlier in the procedure.

The posterior bladder neck, it is more mobile. Your elevation is different when you're not lifting up from the catheter. But I haven't found that to be a significant change. The anterior bladder neck, I couldn't quite master. So I still do it at the start. But the posterior is still normally much the same.

I find when we're teaching, this is one of the hardest parts to teach registrars and even other consultants. Any techniques for this posterior blandic dissection with respect to angles and fibres? Yeah, I'm a big believer of what Vic does in terms of getting some lateral dissection but not going too lateral where the vessels are and flattening it out. And that's basically what I've done here. So I've got a reasonably broad expanse

The surgeon works across a reasonable plane, avoiding lateral dissection into the veins or arteries. A small adenoma is visible in the middle, which the surgeon has dissected underneath. The mucosal lining of the bladder is observed, indicating the angle and thickness of the bladder. The surgeon continues dissection, aiming to leave detrusor fibers on the prostate.

The surgeon continues the dissection until the vertical fibers become visible, indicating the point to move the dissection more anteriorly. With effective retraction using the left hand, the tissue separates smoothly without the need for excessive force or additional tools.

The surgeon begins dissecting through the vertical fibers, revealing a small amount of fat laterally. The dissection is positioned a few millimeters away from the prostate to avoid entering the plane between the prostate, vesicles, and vas, aiming to reach the vas and vesicles on the anterior surface.

If you had someone who was suggestive of seminal vesicle invasion, either clinically or an MRI, does that change how you do this part of the procedure? If they've got bulky disease and I'm sure they've got SV invasion, I'll typically at the very start of the case release the rectum as my first step. And then I can do a much more aggressive dissection around the the seminal vesicles and the vas with very minimal traction on them. So if I'm certain that there's vesicle invasion I do change the technique quite significantly so I can be more aggressive there. Are you still happy to nerve spare patients with seminal vesicle invasion? I think even if we try nerve sparing If we are more aggressive around the vesicles, we do likely cause some damage to the neurovascular bundle at that point, which runs very close.

So I will generally leave more tissue in that area, and I do think the neurovascular bundle will sustain injury from that. So metal clips, you don't find a lot of those come up in the sucker, Jeff, like I used to with all my open cases?

The surgeon uses a clip applier to secure structures near the base of the prostate, carefully maneuvering the dissector from medial to lateral to avoid damaging the neurovascular bundle. The surgeon visually assesses the prostate's orientation and ensures the neurovascular structures are separated from the prostate's base.

The surgeon employs an athermal technique to minimize trauma to neurovascular structures while dissecting through the bladder neck. The dissection is performed lower on one side compared to the other, with careful attention to the prostate's orientation and avoiding direct contact with the prostatic fascia before applying clips.
The surgeon observes minimal bleeding while elevating the vesicles, noting that any initial or back bleeding typically settles quickly, maintaining a clear view of the surgical field.
The surgeon controls the pedicles and tolerates back bleeding while working close to the prostate. Clips are applied on either side in a confined space, minimizing back bleeding.

The surgeon begins the dissection of the seminal vesicles, starting with the vas deferens and working medially. A clear plane is established on the medial edge of the seminal vesicle on both sides, with the rectum covered by a well-released fascia.

The surgeon attempts to join the current dissection with the initial anterior dissection by creating a small tunnel in the middle, preparing to dissect out the vesicles. The camera view remains fixed due to equipment constraints.

The surgeon sharply incises the Denonvilliers' fascia a few millimeters below the prostate, revealing distinct tissue layers. The incision goes through both layers, reaching underneath the Denonvilliers' fascia, with the rectum positioned at a significant distance from the incision site.

The surgeon carefully dissects a plane lateral to the seminal vesicle and medial to the prostatic pedicle, aiming to connect this dissection to the posterior approach. The left hand is positioned just medial to the prostatic pedicle to facilitate this technique.

The surgeon aims to dissect the majority of the neurovascular bundle off the prostate, using Asculap clips to secure and divide visible vessels. The tissue is released down to a small pedicle, where a hemolob clip is applied.

The surgeon incises sharply into the neurovascular bundle on the opposite side, aiming to leave some tissue on the prostate. The vesicles are left intact as the dissection progresses, with the approach adjusted based on the presence of disease at the base of the prostate.

And there are patients where you just don't find a nice playing lateral to the vesicles and it's not easy to get around there into that posterior plane. And if that's the case, So do you just go to the base of the vesicle on this side and is this just going to be a non-nerve sparing side? The difference in how far up on the vesicle you come on this side before you start going through the pedicle?

The surgeon aims to maintain a distance of three to four millimeters from the attachment of the vesicle to the prostate, intending to leave half of the neurovascular tissue with the patient and half on the prostate. The surgeon inspects the lateral border of the prostate and considers using the fourth arm to tense the neurovascular bundle for better identification of the lateral plane.
The surgeon uses the fourth arm to elevate the prostate, enhancing its mobility. The inferior part of the neurovascular bundle is directly divided, with tissue grasped for precise manipulation.

Is there an area where you don't like putting clips too close to the urethra? Is that the reason you're doing that, Geoff? Yeah, I don't like having clips too far down, and control of the clips and being able to clip accurately further along the bundle is quite difficult, particularly on this side when you have a right-sided assistant. So further distally, I'll typically then either put some small titanium clips on once the prostate's out or alternatively over-sew. And back here at the base of the prostate, use Hemolox. But as you can see, I've been doing this technique for a couple of years, but I still don't have a perfect setup on this left side. There's a little bit of clashing. It's a bit harder to get everything exactly where I want it.
The surgeon prepares to divide the vas deferens and seminal vesicles, using clips to secure these structures before excision.

But it's a technique that I enjoy, particularly with the bilateral nerve sparing. And my theory is it takes more of the traction away from the operation, being able to divide the pedicle and release the bundle before this step of the operation. And do you think that the early division of the urethra is helping with early return of continence? I do. I found these things difficult because as you adjust your technique, you're also getting better at the operation.

So if I reverted to a pure anti-grade dissection, better with the operation i'm not sure but certainly the hardest thing i found early on was being able to get early continents that matched what you were getting with your opens and the only real difference i could see was in the open technique That dissection all happened at the very start. I thought potentially that my robotic technique, I was getting more traction injury to the sphincteric mechanism, having that as the last bit that I dissected. And certainly my continence results did improve, whether or not that's from the technique or just from added experience, I don't know.
The surgeon lifts the tissue on one side, preparing to place a couple of clips to complete that side of the dissection. The bundle is visible and being examined.

The surgeon navigates interfacial tissue along the left side of the prostate, ensuring no intrafacial dissection occurs, maintaining proximity to the prostate while preserving the intended surgical planes.

Now the plan to reconstruct, any steps to take through from here? No, I really do what VIP's shown us all and I'll just make sure I'm happy with vascular control first and then I'll do a posterior reconstruction and an anastomosis, really like we see VIP. The only difference I have is with my anastomosis, I now generally just do anterior racket handle reconstructions where I think VIP still does

lateral fish mouth I should say I know sometimes most times you can get the bladder down quite easily but there's the occasion where it is quite tense getting the bladder to the urethra do you revert to a different type of bladder technique that stage or you've just found that you've always been able to get it down? Yeah I really haven't had a lot of trouble ever getting the bladder down I probably sure I've got reasonable mobilization laterally might reduce my pneumoperitoneum a little bit or my Trendelenburg a little bit make sure I do a posterior suture reconstruction and if that's not bringing the bladder down then I would potentially do a posterior racket handle so my masmosis is a little closer but I've never actually had to do that

The surgeon addresses bleeding during a bilateral nerve-sparing prostatectomy by applying compression to the neurovascular bundle using the lateral fascia and controlling distal vessel bleeding with flow seal.

The surgeon observes bleeding on the left side and attempts to manage it by picking up fascial layers. The apical dissection is performed using a straight lens, followed by switching to a 30-degree lens for the bladder neck dissection.

If I'm on the XI, then I stay that way. If I need the view, I'll just flip it to thirty up. With the SI, I'll normally go back to a straight lens to do the anastomosis. You find some patients you see well with a thirty down and I just make the decision on individual merits. But in general principle, you'd still use the zero degree for the, for the apex anastomosis? Generally, yeah. If you had a vessel like that at the apex near a vesca bundle, is that something you would leave if it was at the apex? Normally I'd leave that and at the end I'd put some flow seal and a moist Raytec in and just leave it under a little bit of compression for five minutes and see if that controls it.

I think you can always salvage that situation. These vessels you can't salvage post anastoma. you can wash that out and then put a little suture down there at the very end. We seem to have a shortage of sutures today. So I'm just waiting for a stitch. And you use, what stitch will you use for this, Geoff? Oh, three-o monocryl on an RB-I has been my go-to suture. very familiar with the needle. I think the monofilament's nice with the newer machines compared to braided sutures.

The surgeon observes a small area on the diaphragm where previous attempts to control bleeding were made. The surgical field appears dry, but the surgeon discusses the use of flow seal and moist Raytec for compression if bleeding occurs.
The surgeon holds up the free edge of Denonvilliers' fascia that was cut through with the seminal vesicles, ensuring the area remains dry and free of bleeding.

So do you use quill for the next step? Yeah, I've been really happy with quill as a suture. I have to admit it's a much nicer suture I feel on the tissue than V-lock. There's a needle break off there, so get rid of that quilt and grab another one.

So Jeff, post-operative things while we're having a bit of time here with the next quill. How long do you leave the catheter in for and do you do cystograms routinely? Very rarely do cystograms now. I did cystograms on my first two or three hundred patients. Now I only do them selectively. I do them all the time on salvage cases. But I typically won't do a cystogram I'm happy for the catheter to come out from day five onwards post-operatively. Normally it's a week because I'm back in the hospital privately. So if there's any issues with the patient, I'm there. So it's more a convenience measure than anything else.

I have seen Alex Mottry describing early catheter removal on day two. And I have to admit I've just been a bit anxious about retention rates taking a catheter out that early. Do you have any little triggers not to take a catheter out? Perhaps blood in the catheter or... What would make you not take your catheter out routinely on day seven, five? I just can't remember when that last happened. I guess... The main issue I think would be if the patient had an acute early urine leak and had urine coming out the drain. But I just don't remember the last time I saw that happen. So typically I'll always get the catheter out on day seven. And even if there's a little bit of blood on and off, I still normally take the catheter out.

And just some perioperative things, do they have antibiotics in theatre and what's your antibiotic prophylaxis at time of trial avoid or do you not give them any prophylactic antibiotics? I'll give them Kefzol on induction in theatre and then no further antibiotics. Trial avoids, in the private hospital they normally get a single shot of Keftriaxone when the catheter comes out, whereas where I do it publicly they normally have catheter out with no antibiotic. And I have to admit I haven't noticed any difference in post-operative urinary infection rates that are clinically significant.

The surgeon performs a posterior reconstruction attributed to the Rocco technique, bringing the free edge of denim villias down to the tissue posterior to the urethra and the posterior rhabdo sphincter. The surgeon uses a continuous suture, a modification from the original technique, and prepares for the second layer of the anastomosis.

Do you think using another side procedure such as quill at this particular point is a bit too traumatic on this posterior rapier sphincter? Sorry, V-lock, beg your pardon. V-lock does worry me. I didn't have a lot of trouble with the posterior sutures, but occasionally I'd look at it on the urethra and feel like it was cheese wiring the urethra a little bit, and I didn't like that. So before we had access to the quill, I'd moved back to monocryl from V-lock. But now with the quill, I feel it's a nice suture. You get some hold and purchase on the tissue, but I feel it's quite gentle on it.

If you do find you've got a big middle lobe and you're worried about the ureteric orifice, do you have a technique? Do you use infant feeding tubes? What's your technique under those circumstances at this particular point? Maybe three times ever I've put infant feeding tubes in. I normally just put a haemlock on the end so I don't lose them up the ureter. Generally, I find if you don't have to recept the UO and the Trigone for locally advanced disease, even if you're right on the inter-ureteric bar, you can sew your anastomosis in this fashion without the need for infant feeding tubes or stents, just paying attention to where they are without any trouble.

So it's a rare day that... I'd do anything specific for the UOs. And you wouldn't revert to a posterior reconstruction because you're close to the UOs, you'd still directly suture the way you're doing here? Yeah, early on I'd do some lateral fish mouth closures like Vic does to push the UOs away, but now I find this technique, even if you're right on the interurotech bar, is never a problem.

I suppose if you can see them you're less likely to put a stitch through them? Yeah, exactly. So positioning, do you try and do a stitch every hour on the clock or is it just the way it feels, the way it comes together? Is there any sort of specific technique to how many sutures you'll put in your anastomosis? No, I don't think about it a lot, I have to admit. It probably does end up being somewhat like every hour on the clock. Patients where it's a bit tight and more difficult to get down adequately, I find they end up with more sutures and I've got a shorter suture at the end.

The surgeon completes the posterior layer of the anastomosis, making approximately ten passes through the tissue. Full thickness bites are taken on both the urethra and bladder muscle, ensuring secure suturing without risking pull-through.

wits that you tend to try and take with the urethral suture? I guess what I'm taking here, which I'd estimate is about five mils. I normally like to get a reasonable purchase on the urethra. I certainly don't want them to tear through. I certainly have seen Alex Mottry many years ago taking very tiny little bites and coming up through the dorsal vein. But this is how it taught me and I've been happy with it. I haven't had a reason to do anything different.

You didn't suspend the DVC onto the back of the pubis. Do you think that's something, well, it's obviously something you have changed since you've come back. Any reasons for doing that? Look, if I was tying off the dorsal vein, certainly early on I did suspend the dorsal vein and I think it does provide some better hemostasis when you're dividing the dorsal vein. If it is suspended up, your suture doesn't seem to fall out as much.
The surgeon approaches the front of the anastomosis, taking several bites through the urethra and up through the dorsal vein to enhance stability, without suspending the dorsal vein to the pubis.

Two clips are positioned laterally on the bladder near the anastomosis site. The surgeon assesses their proximity to the anastomosis while suturing, considering whether to remove them if they interfere with the tightness of the anastomosis.

Particularly when I had a patient with one just in the middle of the trigone, away from the anastomosis, I'm not sure if some patients just react badly to it. But it's certainly rare to have a problem. Will you do a double A? Are you going to come back along the same? There you go. Okay. Answered my question. Pretty rough.

The surgeon uses a V-lock suture on the anterior bladder, noting the roughness of the area. The suture is shorter than usual, and the surgeon prepares to take a bite through the dorsal vein, moving to the opposite side.

So normally you're saying you would put some sutures through the dorsal venous complex prior to sewing? I would, as I come across the front wall of the urethra from one side of that anterior racquet handle to the other, I'd put both of those sutures after I come through the urethra up through the sewn dorsal venous and then go back through the bladder and just elevate that anastomosis up towards the dorsal venous complex. But I've obviously taken a few more bites than normal. My suture was shorter, so I'll just do the anastomosis for him.

And that's it. That is a fantastic case, Geoff. Well done. And to Vip, well trained. It's been a honour to have Geoff in Brisbane with us and you've been a wonderful teacher to him and I'm sure many urologists throughout the world. And Vip, again, on behalf of us here in Brisbane and I'm sure on behalf of many urologists throughout the world, thank you for your wonderful passion for urology and for teaching.
Yeah, thanks, Vip. Congratulations. Way to go. Well done, mate. Bye-bye. Cheers, mate. Bye.
